evidence.reference_title: The effectiveness and side effects of pyridostigmine in the treatment of myasthenia gravis: a cross-sectional study.
evidence.snippet: Patients reported a median effectiveness of 60, IQR 28-78 and net benefit of 65, IQR 45-84.

evidence.reference_title: The effectiveness and side effects of pyridostigmine in the treatment of myasthenia gravis: a cross-sectional study.
evidence.snippet: Of all patients currently using pyridostigmine, 91% reported side effects (vs. 55% in the control group).

nodes.description: Autoantibodies (primarily anti-AChR) target the postsynaptic membrane at neuromuscular junctions. Antibodies cause receptor internalization, complement-mediated destruction, and functional blockade, reducing acetylcholine signaling efficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Role of complement in myasthenia gravis.
evidence.snippet: Myasthenia gravis is a prototypic neuroimmune disorder with autoantibodies targeting the acetylcholine receptor complex at the neuromuscular junction.

evidence.reference_title: Myasthenia gravis: the role of complement at the neuromuscular junction.
evidence.snippet: Approximately 74-88% of patients with gMG have acetylcholine receptor (AChR) autoantibodies.

nodes.description: Anti-AChR antibodies activate the classical complement pathway, leading to membrane attack complex formation and destruction of the postsynaptic membrane architecture.

evidence.reference_title: Myasthenia gravis: the role of complement at the neuromuscular junction.
evidence.snippet: activation and amplification of complement results in the formation of membrane attack complexes (MACs), lipophilic proteins that damage cell membranes.

evidence.reference_title: Myasthenia gravis: the role of complement at the neuromuscular junction.
evidence.snippet: Studies in animals lacking specific complement proteins have confirmed that MAC formation is required to induce experimental autoimmune MG (EAMG) and NMJ damage.

evidence.reference_title: Role of complement in myasthenia gravis.
evidence.snippet: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are present in the majority of patients and are of IgG1 and IgG3 subtypes which can activate the complement system. The complement involvement plays a major role in the neuromuscular junction damage.

nodes.description: Prednisone for immunosuppression, mainstay of therapy.
Complement inhibitor (anti-C5), approved for refractory AChR+ MG.

evidence.reference_title: Safety and efficacy of eculizumab in anti-acetylcholine receptor antibody-positive refractory generalised myasthenia gravis (REGAIN): a phase 3, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicentre study.
evidence.snippet: Results from a phase 2 study suggested that eculizumab, a terminal complement inhibitor, produced clinically meaningful improvements in patients with anti-acetylcholine receptor antibody-positive refractory generalised myasthenia gravis.

evidence.reference_title: Safety and efficacy of eculizumab in anti-acetylcholine receptor antibody-positive refractory generalised myasthenia gravis (REGAIN): a phase 3, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicentre study.
evidence.snippet: Myasthenia gravis exacerbations were reported by six (10%) patients in the eculizumab group and 15 (24%) in the placebo group.

nodes.description: FcRn antagonist approved for generalized AChR-positive MG; reduces pathogenic IgG levels by blocking neonatal Fc receptor recycling.

evidence.reference_title: FcRN receptor antagonists in the management of myasthenia gravis
evidence.snippet: Efgartigimod is already approved for the treatment of generalized MG

evidence.reference_title: FcRN receptor antagonists in the management of myasthenia gravis
evidence.snippet: FcRn receptors prevent the catabolism of IgG by impeding their lysosomal degradation and facilitating their extracellular release at physiological pH, consequently extending the IgG half-life.

nodes.description: Immunomodulation, used for exacerbations and crisis.
Rapid removal of antibodies, used for myasthenic crisis.

evidence.reference_title: Anti-MuSK antibody myasthenia gravis: clinical findings and response to treatment in two large cohorts.
evidence.snippet: Plasma exchange (PLEX) produced improvement in 93%, whereas only 61% improved after intravenous immunoglobulin.

nodes.description: Anti-CD20 antibody, effective in refractory cases.
FcRn antagonist for generalized AChR-positive MG; reduces pathogenic IgG via neonatal Fc receptor blockade.

evidence.reference_title: FcRN receptor antagonists in the management of myasthenia gravis
evidence.snippet: rozanolixizumab is under review by health authorities

nodes.description: Surgical removal of thymus, improves outcomes in appropriate patients.

evidence.reference_title: Randomized Trial of Thymectomy in Myasthenia Gravis.
evidence.snippet: Patients who underwent thymectomy had a lower time-weighted average Quantitative Myasthenia Gravis score over a 3-year period than those who received prednisone alone (6.15 vs. 8.99, P<0.001)

evidence.reference_title: Randomized Trial of Thymectomy in Myasthenia Gravis.
evidence.snippet: Fewer patients in the thymectomy group than in the prednisone-only group required immunosuppression with azathioprine (17% vs. 48%, P<0.001) or were hospitalized for exacerbations (9% vs. 37%, P<0.001).

nodes.description: Thymic hyperplasia (65-75% of patients) or thymoma (10-15%) is common. The thymus may serve as the site of autoimmunization, containing myoid cells expressing AChR that activate autoreactive T cells.

evidence.reference_title: The thymus in myasthenia gravis: Site of "innate autoimmunity"?
evidence.snippet: The thymus plays a major role in the pathogenesis of MG with anti-AChR antibodies: it shows marked pathologic alterations (hyperplastic or tumoral) in most AChR-positive patients and contains the elements required to initiate and sustain an autoimmune reaction (AChR autoantigen, AChR-specific T cells, and autoantibody-secreting plasma cells).

evidence.reference_title: The thymus in myasthenia gravis: Site of "innate autoimmunity"?
evidence.snippet: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder caused, in most cases, by autoantibodies against components of the neuromuscular junction, frequently the acetylcholine receptor (AChR), and less often the muscle-specific kinase receptor.
